response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a native-born American woman who's been using social media for years, I've seen firsthand how it can turn into this chaotic mess. It's not just about the big lies or conspiracy theories that pop up; even little things like altered photos of my kids at 'perfect' beaches make me anxious. My anxiety makes me hyper-aware of the filter bubbles we create — sometimes you're seeing curated perfection because an algorithm thinks you'll believe it, and honestly, I don't want to be in that echo chamber.\n\nI think managing fake content isn't just about fact-checking anymore; it's like herding cats when they've all been fed sugar. You can't block everything without feeling isolated. My hobby of photography helps me see how easily things are manipulated — a simple Photoshop tutorial shows you how easy it is to alter an image.\n\nSo, I suggest starting with the basics: double-check sources and be skeptical about anything that seems too good or too perfect. And don't forget to mute the outrage if it's stressing you out because my anxiety level isn't low; moderate means I still feel stress sometimes.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Based on my persona as a moderately anxious, educated woman with a barista job in Los Angeles who spends several hours daily on social media, I derived that fake content often feels personal and distressing. The combination of high conscientiousness (wanting to manage effectively) and moderate openness led me to consider both technical solutions like source checking and emotional coping strategies like muting stressors.",
  "tone": "Reflective with a touch of weary pragmatism, matching the persona's age and anxiety level.",
  "citations": ["Self-experience", "Photoshop tutorials"],
  "used_persona_fields":["age","gender","psychological_feature","hobbies"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a native-born American woman who's been using social media for years, I've seen firsthand how it can turn into this chaotic mess. It's not just about the big lies or conspiracy theories that pop up; even little things like altered photos of my kids at 'perfect' beaches make me anxious. My anxiety makes me hyper-aware of the filter bubbles we create — sometimes you're seeing curated perfection because an algorithm thinks you'll believe it, and honestly, I don't want to be in that echo chamber.

I think managing fake content isn't just about fact-checking anymore; it's like herding cats when they've all been fed sugar. You can't block everything without feeling isolated. My hobby of photography helps me see how easily things are manipulated — a simple Photoshop tutorial shows you how easy it is to alter an image.

So, I suggest starting with the basics: double-check sources and be skeptical about anything that seems too good or too perfect. And don't forget to mute the outrage if it's stressing you out because my anxiety level isn't low; moderate means I still feel stress sometimes.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Based on my persona as a moderately anxious, educated woman with a barista job in Los Angeles who spends several hours daily on social media, I derived that fake content often feels personal and distressing. The combination of high conscientiousness (wanting to manage effectively) and moderate openness led me to consider both technical solutions like source checking and emotional coping strategies like muting stressors.
response_parsed.tone: Reflective with a touch of weary pragmatism, matching the persona's age and anxiety level.
